title: NEURAL NETWORKS WITH TRAINABLE MATRIX ACTIVATION FUNCTIONS [PDF]

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## NEURAL NETWORKS WITH TRAINABLE MATRIX ACTIVATION FUNCTIONS

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

The training process of neural networks usually optimizes weights and bias parameters of linear transformations, while nonlinear activation functions are prespecified and fixed. This work develops a systematic approach to constructing matrix activation functions whose entries are generalized from ReLU. The activation is based on matrix-vector multiplications using only scalar multiplications and comparisons. The proposed activation functions depend on parameters that are trained along with the weights and bias vectors. Neural networks based on this approach are simple and efficient and are shown to be robust in numerical experiments.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

In recent decades, deep neural networks (DNNs) have achieved significant successes in many fields such as computer vision and natural language processing (Voulodimos et al., 2018), (Otter et al., 2018). The DNN surrogate model is constructed using recursive composition of linear transformations and nonlinear activation functions. To achieve good performance, it is essential to choose activation functions suitable for specific applications. In practice, Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU) is one of the most popular activation functions for its simplicity and efficiency. A drawback of ReLU is the presence of vanishing gradient in the training process, known as dying ReLU problem (Lu et al., 2019). Several relatively new activation approaches are proposed to overcome this problem, e.g., the simple Leaky ReLU, and Piecewise Linear Unit (PLU) (Nicolae, 2018), Softplus (Glorot et al., 2011), Exponential Linear Unit (ELU) (Clevert et al., 2016), Scaled Exponential Linear Unit (SELU) (Klambauer et al., 2017), Gaussian Error Linear Unit (GELU) (Hendrycks & Gimpel, 2016).

Although the aforementioned activation functions are shown to be competitive in benchmark tests, they are still fixed nonlinear functions. In a DNN structure, it is often hard to determine a priori the optimal activation function for a specific application. In this paper, we shall generalize ReLU and introduce arbitrary trainable matrix activation functions. The effectiveness of the proposed approach is validated using function approximation examples and well-known benchmark datasets such as CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100. There are a few classical works on adaptively tuning of parameters in the training process, e.g., the parametric ReLU (He et al., 2015b). However, our adaptive matrix activation functions are shown to be competitive and more robust in those experiments.

## REMARK

_The activation functions used in TMAF neural network are not piecewise constants. Instead, TMAF activation is realized using matrix-vector multiplication, where entries of those matrices are trainable piecewise constants. There exist several trainable activation functions in the literature, e.g., Parametric ReLU (He et al., 2015b), Pad´e Activation Unit (PAU) (Molina et al., 2020), Piecewise Linear Unit (PWLU) (Zhou et al., 2021), Swish (Ramachandran et al., 2017), adaptive rational functions (Boull´e et al., 2020), adaptive splines (Bohra et al., 2020), adaptive activation functions for physics informed neural networks (ada, 2020). We point out that TMAF is different from the aforementioned activation approaches. One might think TMAF is similar to PWLU because they are both defined piecewise. However, by construction TMAF includes discontinuous functions as special cases (see Figure 1) while PWLU always results in a_ continuous _piecewise linear function with trainable slopes._

Starting from the diagonal activation _Dℓ_ , we can go one step further to construct more general activation matrices. First we note that _Dℓ_ could be viewed as a nonlinear operator _Tℓ_ : [ _C_ (R _[d]_ )] _[n][ℓ] →_ [ _C_ (R _[d]_ )] _[n][ℓ]_ , where

There seems to be no special reason, aside from keeping the computational cost as low as possible, to consider only diagonal operators _D_ . A more ambitious approach is to consider a trainable nonlinear activation _operator_ determined by more general matrices, for example, by a tri-diagonal operator

for _x ∈_ R _[d] ._ The diagonal entry _αℓ_ is given in equation 6 while the off-diagonal entries _βℓ_ , _γℓ_ are piecewise constant functions defined in a fashion similar to _αℓ_ . Theoretically speaking, even trainable full matrix activation is possible despite of potentially huge training cost. In summary, a DNN based on trainable nonlinear activation operators _{Tℓ}[L] ℓ_ =1[reads]

As we indicated above, TMAF can be used with any matrix and this will lead to a great flexibility in training and approximation. In fact, if a sparse matrix _D_ is used, so that the matrix-vector multiplication is proportional to the number of rows in the matrix, then the resulting algorithm will be computationally efficient. We can choose p-diagonal matrices, as well as matrices with prescribed sparsity patterns. Clearly, based on the simple examples we have considered here, one may conclude that such possibilities, when investigated in depth, can increase the robustness and the accuracy of the TMAF-NN.

diagonal TMAF as described in Subsection 3.1. Numerical results are shown in Figures 7a, 7b and Table 3. We observe that performance of the TMAF and the ReLU networks are similar. Such a behavior clearly should be expected for a simple dataset such as MNIST.

For the CIFAR-10 dataset, we use the ResNet18 network structure provided by (He et al., 2015a). The activation functions are still ReLU and the diagonal TMAF used in Subsection 3.1. Numerical results are presented in Figures 8a, 8b and Table 3. Those parameters given in (Paszke et al., 2019) are already tuned well with respect to ReLU. Nevertheless, TMAF still produces smaller errors in the training process and returns better classification results in the evaluation stage, see Table 3.

For the CIFAR-100 dataset, we use the ResNet34 network structure provided by (He et al., 2015a) with the ReLU and TMAF activation functions in Subsection 3.1. Numerical results are presented in Figures 9a and 9b. In the training process, TMAF again outperforms the classical ReLU network.

It is possible to improve the performance of TMAF applied to those benchmark datasets. The key point is to select suitable intervals in _αℓ_ to optimize the performance. A simple strategy is to let those intervals in equation 6 be varying and adjusted in the training (or the evaluation) process, which will be investigated in our future research.

progressive__claude-opus-4.7.label: Progressive (claude-opus-4.7)
progressive__claude-opus-4.7.overall_feedback: This paper proposes "Trainable Matrix Activation Functions" (TMAF), a generalization of ReLU in which the activation is expressed as a diagonal matrix-vector product whose diagonal entries are piecewise-constant functions with trainable step values. While the core idea—learning activation functions jointly with weights—is reasonable and the matrix-multiplication reformulation is a clean pedagogical framing, the novelty appears quite limited: the construction is essentially a trainable piecewise-constant activation, which is closely related to a substantial body of existing trainable activation work (PReLU, PAU, PWLU, Swish, adaptive splines/rationals, etc.) that the authors acknowledge but distinguish only superficially (primarily by noting TMAF can be discontinuous, which is arguably a disadvantage rather than an advantage, as it could harm gradient-based optimization—an issue the paper does not address). The presentation also has clarity issues: key equations appear to be missing or poorly rendered, the grid points s_j are chosen a priori without discussion of how, the treatment of gradients through discontinuous step functions is not explained, and the motivation jumps quickly from ReLU's "dying" problem to matrix activations without clearly articulating why the proposed form specifically addresses it. Most critically, the claim that TMAF "is clearly not worse than traditional DNNs" because it subsumes ReLU/Leaky ReLU as special cases conflates expressivity with trainability and empirical performance, and the abstract's promise of robustness will need strong, carefully controlled experiments on CIFAR-10/100 (with fair parameter budgets and comparisons against the many cited trainable activations) to be convincing. Overall, the paper in its current form reads as an incremental contribution whose distinctiveness, theoretical justification, and optimization considerations require substantially more development.

comments.title: Unjustified claim that TMAF is 'clearly not worse' than traditional DNNs
comments.quote: Since ReLU and Leaky ReLU are included by our DNN as special cases, the proposed DNN is clearly not worse than the traditional ones in practice.
comments.explanation: Containment of ReLU/Leaky ReLU in the hypothesis class only guarantees that in principle there exist parameter settings matching traditional networks. In practice, optimization landscapes, initialization, and training dynamics mean a larger hypothesis class can perform worse on a fixed training budget. The phrase 'clearly not worse … in practice' overstates what the inclusion argument implies; it should be qualified (e.g., 'in terms of expressive capacity' rather than 'in practice').

comments.title: Piecewise-constant α approximating continuous functions 'to arbitrary accuracy' — justification gap for activation role
comments.quote: Since piecewise constant functions can approximate a continuous function within arbitrarily high accuracy, we choose _αℓ_ to be the following step function
comments.explanation: The motivational sentence says piecewise constants can approximate continuous functions arbitrarily well, and therefore α_ℓ is chosen to be a step function. This is logically a non-sequitur as stated: α_ℓ here is not being used to approximate some target continuous activation — it plays the role of the diagonal entries of D_ℓ, and the resulting component-wise activation σ_ℓ(t) = α_ℓ(t)·t is piecewise linear (with possible jumps), not an approximation to a single fixed continuous activation. The justification conflates 'approximating continuous functions by step functions' with 'step-valued multipliers yield a good activation family.'

comments.title: Tri-diagonal TMAF interval specification is inconsistent/unclear
comments.explanation: I tried to reconstruct the interval partition for β_ℓ. With h=0.02 and k ranging over 0..99, the subintervals (−2.01 + kh, −2.01 + (k+1)h] tile (−2.01, −0.03]. But the first interval listed is (−∞, −2.01 + 99h] = (−∞, −0.03], which overlaps the entire range of the k-indexed subintervals rather than being the left tail (−∞, −2.01]. It appears a typo: the leftmost interval should likely be (−∞, −2.01] to be consistent with the partitioning pattern used for α_ℓ. A reader cannot reproduce the tri-diagonal TMAF setup from this description without guessing the intended endpoints.

comments.title: MNIST input dimension stated as 764 instead of 784
comments.quote: with 10 neurons per layer (except at the first layer _n_ 0 = 764)
comments.explanation: MNIST images are 28×28 = 784 pixels, so the input dimension should be 784, not 764. No custom preprocessing is mentioned. This is a factual numerical error that affects reproducibility — a reader trying to reproduce the experiment would be confused about the actual input size.

comments.title: Sparse-matrix cost claim is imprecise
comments.quote: if a sparse matrix _D_ is used, so that the matrix-vector multiplication is proportional to the number of rows in the matrix, then the resulting algorithm will be computationally efficient.
comments.explanation: The cost of a sparse matrix-vector product scales with the number of nonzeros, not the number of rows per se (unless each row has O(1) nonzeros, e.g., p-diagonal with fixed p). The statement as written is only correct under the implicit assumption of bounded row sparsity. A careful reader can infer the intent from the surrounding mention of p-diagonal matrices, but the phrasing is loose.

comments.title: CIFAR-100 results missing from Table 3 despite being discussed
comments.quote: For the CIFAR-100 dataset, we use the ResNet34 network structure provided by (He et al., 2015a) with the ReLU and TMAF activation functions in Subsection 3.1. Numerical results are presented in Figures 9a and 9b. In the training process, TMAF again outperforms the classical ReLU network.
comments.explanation: The text describes CIFAR-100 experiments and claims TMAF outperforms ReLU, but Table 3 only contains rows for MNIST and CIFAR-10 — CIFAR-100 evaluation accuracy is absent. The prose only says 'In the training process, TMAF again outperforms' which is notably weaker than the CIFAR-10 claim about evaluation accuracy. A careful reader would wonder: does TMAF outperform ReLU on CIFAR-100 at evaluation (i.e., generalization), or only on the training loss? Without the evaluation numbers in the table, the claim is unsupported for CIFAR-100 generalization, and the asymmetric phrasing hints that evaluation accuracy may not actually favor TMAF.

progressive_original__claude-opus-4.7.label: Progressive Original (claude-opus-4.7)
progressive_original__claude-opus-4.7.overall_feedback: This paper proposes "Trainable Matrix Activation Functions" (TMAF), a generalization of ReLU in which the activation is expressed as a diagonal matrix-vector product whose diagonal entries are piecewise-constant functions with trainable step values. While the core idea—learning activation functions jointly with weights—is reasonable and the matrix-multiplication reformulation is a clean pedagogical framing, the novelty appears quite limited: the construction is essentially a trainable piecewise-constant activation, which is closely related to a substantial body of existing trainable activation work (PReLU, PAU, PWLU, Swish, adaptive splines/rationals, etc.) that the authors acknowledge but distinguish only superficially (primarily by noting TMAF can be discontinuous, which is arguably a disadvantage rather than an advantage, as it could harm gradient-based optimization—an issue the paper does not address). The presentation also has clarity issues: key equations appear to be missing or poorly rendered, the grid points s_j are chosen a priori without discussion of how, the treatment of gradients through discontinuous step functions is not explained, and the motivation jumps quickly from ReLU's "dying" problem to matrix activations without clearly articulating why the proposed form specifically addresses it. Most critically, the claim that TMAF "is clearly not worse than traditional DNNs" because it subsumes ReLU/Leaky ReLU as special cases conflates expressivity with trainability and empirical performance, and the abstract's promise of robustness will need strong, carefully controlled experiments on CIFAR-10/100 (with fair parameter budgets and comparisons against the many cited trainable activations) to be convincing. Overall, the paper in its current form reads as an incremental contribution whose distinctiveness, theoretical justification, and optimization considerations require substantially more development.

comments.title: Unjustified claim that TMAF is 'not worse' than traditional DNNs
comments.quote: Since ReLU and Leaky ReLU are included by our DNN as special cases, the proposed DNN is clearly not worse than the traditional ones in practice.
comments.explanation: Initially this sounds reasonable: if the TMAF family contains ReLU/Leaky ReLU as special cases, then in principle the optimal TMAF should match ReLU performance. However, 'not worse ... in practice' is a stronger claim than 'not worse in principle.' In practice, having a larger hypothesis class can hurt generalization or optimization (more parameters, harder loss landscape, risk of overfitting). The authors provide no theoretical or empirical argument that the training procedure actually realizes this lower bound on every problem. This is a mild overclaim — the correct statement would be 'not worse in the best case' or 'has at least the representational capacity of.'

comments.title: Piecewise-constant α approximating continuous functions 'to arbitrary accuracy' — justification gap for activation role
comments.quote: Since piecewise constant functions can approximate a continuous function within arbitrarily high accuracy, we choose _αℓ_ to be the following step function
comments.explanation: The motivational sentence says piecewise constants can approximate continuous functions arbitrarily well, and therefore α_ℓ is chosen to be a step function. This is logically a non-sequitur as stated: α_ℓ here is not being used to approximate some target continuous activation — it plays the role of the diagonal entries of D_ℓ, and the resulting component-wise activation σ_ℓ(t) = α_ℓ(t)·t is piecewise linear (with possible jumps), not an approximation to a single fixed continuous activation. The justification conflates 'approximating continuous functions by step functions' with 'step-valued multipliers yield a good activation family.' A careful reader may find the motivation underspecified, though the construction itself is clear from the subsequent equations.

comments.title: Definition of the step function α_ℓ is referenced but not shown in passage

The actual displayed equation defining α_ℓ is not shown in the extracted passage (likely an equation image that didn't OCR). This is a passage-boundary/OCR artifact rather than an authorial omission, so I will not flag it as a real issue — just noting it for completeness. (Not flagging.)

comments.explanation: MNIST images are 28×28 = 784 pixels, so the input dimension should be 784, not 764. Initially I wondered if this could be some custom preprocessing (e.g., cropping), but no such preprocessing is mentioned, and the paper says 'classifying images in the MNIST' set using standard MNIST. This is almost certainly a typo (764 vs 784), but it is a factual numerical error that affects reproducibility — a reader trying to reproduce the experiment would be confused about the actual input size. comments.title: CIFAR-100 results missing from Table 3 despite being discussed
comments.quote: For the CIFAR-100 dataset, we use the ResNet34 network structure provided by (He et al., 2015a) with the ReLU and TMAF activation functions in Subsection 3.1. Numerical results are presented in Figures 9a and 9b. In the training process, TMAF again outperforms the classical ReLU network.
comments.explanation: The text describes CIFAR-100 experiments and claims TMAF outperforms ReLU, but Table 3 only contains rows for MNIST and CIFAR-10 — CIFAR-100 evaluation accuracy is absent. The prose only says 'In the training process, TMAF again outperforms' which is notably weaker than the CIFAR-10 claim about evaluation accuracy. A careful reader would wonder: does TMAF outperform ReLU on CIFAR-100 at evaluation (i.e., generalization), or only on the training loss? Without the evaluation numbers in the table, the claim is unsupported for CIFAR-100 generalization, and the asymmetric phrasing hints that evaluation accuracy may not actually favor TMAF. This is a real gap in the experimental evidence as presented.

comments.title: Unsupported general claim that TMAF is 'clearly not worse' than traditional DNNs
comments.quote: Since ReLU and Leaky ReLU are included by our DNN as special cases, the proposed DNN is clearly not worse than the traditional ones in practice.
comments.explanation: This claim appears in Section 2 (summarized above) but is relevant context for the experimental section. The fact that ReLU is a special case of the TMAF parameterization does not imply TMAF is 'not worse ... in practice' — optimization of a larger parameter space can easily yield worse practical results due to harder optimization landscapes, overfitting, or initialization issues. The authors conflate representational inclusion with practical performance. While this is in an earlier section, it is worth flagging since it frames the experimental comparisons. However, since this was in a prior section not the passage being checked, I'll note it only as context — the passage under review does not itself repeat this overclaim.

progressive__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive (gpt-5.5)
progressive__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper proposes an interesting and potentially useful idea—learning layer-wise, piecewise-defined multiplicative activation parameters that generalize ReLU/Leaky ReLU—but the current presentation raises substantial concerns about novelty, clarity, and technical rigor. Conceptually, the “matrix activation” terminology seems overstated in the initial construction, since the activation is diagonal and effectively amounts to a trainable piecewise-linear scalar activation of the form \(x \mapsto \alpha(x)x\); the relationship to existing adaptive/piecewise activations such as PReLU, APL, maxout, splines, rational activations, and PWLU needs a much sharper and more convincing comparison. The mathematical exposition is also difficult to follow: notation is often corrupted or inconsistent, equations are missing or poorly rendered, and key definitions—especially how the step parameters are trained, whether thresholds are fixed or learnable, and how gradients behave at discontinuities—are not explained with sufficient precision. Several claims are too strong or unsupported, e.g., that the method is “clearly not worse” than ReLU because it contains ReLU as a special case; this ignores optimization dynamics, parameterization effects, regularization, and possible instability from discontinuous activations. The paper would benefit from a clearer statement of the actual contribution, a principled analysis of expressivity/optimization/gradient behavior, and careful empirical controls against close baselines with comparable parameter counts. Overall, the idea may be worth exploring, but in its current form the beginning of the paper lacks the precision and positioning needed for a strong ICLR submission.

comments.title: Dying ReLU is conflated with the broader vanishing-gradient problem
comments.quote: A drawback of ReLU is the presence of vanishing gradient in the training process, known as dying ReLU problem (Lu et al., 2019).
comments.explanation: The authors are motivating trainable activations by pointing to a well-known weakness of ReLU, but the wording merges two related but distinct phenomena. The standard vanishing-gradient problem usually refers to gradients becoming very small through repeated chain-rule multiplication in deep networks, especially with saturating nonlinearities. The dying ReLU problem is more specific: a ReLU unit can enter a regime where its preactivation is negative for essentially all inputs, so its local derivative is zero and the unit stops updating. ReLU was popular partly because it mitigates classical saturation-based vanishing gradients on the positive side. As written, the sentence could mislead readers about the mechanism being addressed.

comments.title: “Arbitrary trainable matrix activation functions” overstates the construction presented here
comments.quote: In this paper, we shall generalize ReLU and introduce arbitrary trainable matrix activation functions.
comments.explanation: The high-level goal is clear, and later context discusses going beyond diagonal matrices to tridiagonal and potentially sparse or full matrix operators. Still, in the formal construction in this passage the activation is specifically a diagonal matrix whose entries are generated by a layerwise scalar step function with fixed grid points and trainable step values. That is a useful and flexible family, but it is not arbitrary in the usual mathematical sense: the entries are constrained by the chosen piecewise-constant parameterization, are shared through \(\alpha_\ell\), and the grid is later fixed in experiments. The wording therefore overclaims what has actually been defined and trained.

comments.title: Piecewise-constant approximation claim needs domain and norm qualifications
comments.quote: Since piecewise constant functions can approximate a continuous function within arbitrarily high accuracy, we choose _αℓ_ to be the following step function
comments.explanation: The intended justification is that step functions are a natural, computationally cheap way to parameterize the multiplier \(\alpha_\ell\), and on compact intervals they can approximate continuous functions uniformly if the partition is sufficiently fine. However, the statement as written is too broad. Uniform approximation of an arbitrary continuous function on all of \(\mathbb{R}\) by a finite piecewise-constant function is not generally possible without additional assumptions, and the relevant approximation norm is not specified. In the actual construction \(\alpha_\ell\) is defined on an unbounded real line with finitely many intervals, including tails, so the standard compact-domain result does not apply directly. This is fixable by saying, for example, “on a compact range of preactivations” or “in an empirical/Lp sense over the data distribution.”

comments.title: Hypothesis-class inclusion does not imply practical performance is “clearly not worse”
comments.quote: Since ReLU and Leaky ReLU are included by our DNN as special cases, the proposed DNN is clearly not worse than the traditional ones in practice.
comments.explanation: At the level of representable functions, a parameterized family containing ReLU and Leaky ReLU has at least the same best-case approximation capacity, assuming the architecture and objective are otherwise comparable. What does not follow is the practical claim. Neural-network training is nonconvex, and adding trainable activation parameters can change optimization dynamics, initialization sensitivity, regularization behavior, and effective learning rates. Therefore, inclusion of ReLU/Leaky ReLU as special cases only supports a statement about the optimal attainable loss over the enlarged function class, not that the trained model will be no worse in practice.

comments.title: Activation operator is not generally a map from continuous functions to continuous functions
comments.quote: First we note that _Dℓ_ could be viewed as a nonlinear operator _Tℓ_ : [ _C_ (R _[d]_ )] _[n][ℓ] →_ [ _C_ (R _[d]_ )] _[n][ℓ]_ , where
comments.explanation: The authors reinterpret the diagonal activation as an operator acting on vector-valued layer functions, which is useful for moving from diagonal to tridiagonal or full activation matrices. However, the earlier definition of \(\alpha_\ell\) is a trainable step function, and the paper explicitly emphasizes that TMAF can include discontinuous activation functions. If \(u\in C(\mathbb R^d)\) crosses one of the step thresholds \(s_j\), then \(\alpha_\ell(u(x))u(x)\) is generally discontinuous unless special compatibility conditions hold at that threshold. Thus \(T_\ell\) does not, in general, map \([C(\mathbb R^d)]^{n_\ell}\) into itself. The intended operator could instead be defined on all functions, measurable functions, or piecewise-continuous functions.

comments.title: Sparse matrix cost claim needs bounded sparsity, not just sparsity
comments.quote: In fact, if a sparse matrix _D_ is used, so that the matrix-vector multiplication is proportional to the number of rows in the matrix, then the resulting algorithm will be computationally efficient.
comments.explanation: Diagonal, tridiagonal, or fixed-band activation matrices can keep the activation cost linear in the layer width. That is true for matrices with a uniformly bounded number of nonzeros per row, such as \(p\)-diagonal matrices with fixed \(p\). But the sentence says this follows from using a sparse matrix. In standard numerical linear algebra, sparse matrix-vector multiplication costs \(O(\operatorname{nnz}(D))\), not automatically \(O(n_\ell)\). A sparse matrix can still have, for example, \(O(n_\ell\log n_\ell)\) or \(O(n_\ell^{3/2})\) nonzeros. The cost statement should explicitly require \(O(n_\ell)\) nonzeros or bounded row degree.

comments.title: Robustness and accuracy conclusion is stronger than the evidence in this section
comments.quote: Clearly, based on the simple examples we have considered here, one may conclude that such possibilities, when investigated in depth, can increase the robustness and the accuracy of the TMAF-NN.
comments.explanation: The authors hedge that these possibilities need to be investigated in depth, and the examples suggest that richer activation matrices may be promising. Still, the word “Clearly” and the conclusion about increased robustness and accuracy are not fully supported by the material here. At this point the paper has described the possibility of diagonal, tridiagonal, and sparse patterns, and later experiments include only a limited set of comparisons. Robustness is also not formally defined in this passage. A more defensible wording would be that these possibilities may improve robustness and accuracy and merit further study.

comments.title: Extension to arbitrary activations is not generally a matrix-vector activation in the same sense
comments.quote: _Our observation also applies to an activation function σ other than ReLU. For example, we may rescale σ_ ( _x_ ) _to obtain σ_ ( _ωi,ℓx_ ) _using a set of constants {ωi,ℓ}_ 1 _≤i≤nℓ,_ 1 _≤ℓ≤L varying layer by layer and neuron by neuron.
comments.explanation: The intended idea seems to be a trainable per-neuron rescaling of a standard activation, which is a reasonable adaptive-activation construction. However, the earlier matrix-activation observation for ReLU works because \(\operatorname{ReLU}(t)=d(t)t\) with \(d(t)\in\{0,1\}\). For a general activation \(\sigma\), representing \(\sigma(\omega t)\) as a diagonal matrix times the input would require a diagonal entry \(\sigma(\omega t)/t\), which may be undefined at \(t=0\) and cannot represent activations with \(\sigma(0)\ne 0\) in the form \(D(t)t\). Many common activations, such as Softplus or sigmoid-type functions, do not naturally fit this without extra qualifications. If the authors only mean ordinary componentwise scaled activations, then it is not the same matrix-vector observation unless the necessary conditions are stated.

comments.title: Training protocol omits key reproducibility details
comments.quote: In each experiment, we use the same learning rates, stochastic optimization methods, and number NE of epochs (optimization iterations). In particular, the learning rate is 1e-4 from epoch 1 to[NE] 2[and 1e-5 is used from epoch][NE] 2[+ 1][ to][ NE][.][The optimization] method is ADAM (Kingma & Ba (2015)) based on mini-batches of size 500.
comments.explanation: The passage gives useful high-level training information: optimizer, batch size, and learning-rate schedule. However, the actual value of \(NE\) is not specified, and neither are initializations for the trainable step values \(t_{\ell,j}\). For TMAF, these initialization choices can materially affect optimization, especially because the interval assignments are non-smooth and only the step values are trained. For the image experiments, preprocessing, augmentation, and weight initialization are also important for reproducing CIFAR/MNIST results. The omission does not invalidate the method, but it makes the numerical comparisons difficult to reproduce or assess fairly.

comments.title: Gaussian equal-probability interval claim is numerically inaccurate as stated
comments.quote: For TMAF _Dℓ_ in equation 5, the function _αℓ_ uses intervals ( _−∞, −_ 1 _._ 4), ( _−_ 1 _._ 4 _, −_ 0 _._ 92], ( _−_ 0 _._ 92 _, −_ 0 _._ 56], ( _−_ 0 _._ 56 _, −_ 0 _._ 26], ( _−_ 0 _._ 26 _,_ 0], (0 _,_ 0 _._ 26], (0 _._ 26 _,_ 0 _._ 56], (0 _._ 56 _,_ 0 _._ 92], (0 _._ 92 _,_ 1 _._ 4], (1 _._ 4 _, ∞_ ) such that probability over each of the ten intervals is 0.1 with respect to Gaussian distribution.
comments.explanation: The intent is clear: choose grid intervals roughly matched to Gaussian quantiles, likely because BatchNorm makes preactivations approximately standardized. But the listed cut points are not the standard normal deciles. Equal 0.1-probability bins would use approximate cutoffs \(\pm1.2816\), \(\pm0.8416\), \(\pm0.5244\), \(\pm0.2533\), and 0. The provided values are close in the middle but noticeably off in the tails: for example, \(P(Z>1.4)\approx0.081\), not 0.1. If these are only heuristic rounded bins, the prose should say approximately; if exact equal-mass Gaussian bins are intended, the cutoffs should be corrected.

comments.explanation: The relevant experimental setup is that the diagonal TMAF, ReLU, and parametric ReLU oscillatory-function networks use 400 neurons per hidden layer, while the tridiagonal TMAF uses 300. The authors may have intended this to balance cost, since tridiagonal activations add off-diagonal interactions. Still, the passage does not explain whether parameter counts, FLOPs, or wall-clock costs are being controlled. TMAF also adds trainable activation parameters relative to ReLU/parametric ReLU. comments.title: Oscillatory-function results conflate training loss, approximation error, and visual approximation quality
comments.quote: Table 2: Error comparison for sin(100 _πx_ ) + cos(50 _πx_ ) + sin( _πx_ )
comments.explanation: The surrounding text uses Table 2 and Figure 4b to support claims about approximation quality. However, the table heading in context reports final loss, and Figure 4b is described as a training loss comparison, not an approximation plot. Thus the numbers appear to be final training losses, not necessarily test errors or approximation errors over the whole domain. This matters because the paper’s formal loss in equation 3 is a sum over the training data, while the table values appear more like a normalized MSE or averaged loss. Figure 5 directly shows approximation curves, but Figure 4b does not. The issue is fixable by stating whether the metric is averaged MSE, summed squared error, training error, or test/grid error, and by referring to Figure 5 for visual approximation quality and Figure 4b/Table 2 for training loss.

comments.title: Claim that ReLU networks clearly need many more neurons is stronger than the evidence shown
comments.quote: To capture the high frequency, ReLU-type neural networks are clearly required to use much more neurons, introducing significantly amount of weight and bias parameters.
comments.explanation: The empirical observation is plausible: piecewise-linear ReLU networks often need many linear regions to represent high-frequency oscillations accurately, and the shown ReLU/Para-ReLU runs fail on this target. However, the context does not provide a theoretical lower bound or a systematic width/depth study. The claim is based on one architecture and training setup, so the failure could also reflect optimization, initialization, learning-rate schedule, depth/width choice, or insufficient training rather than an inherent requirement for much more neurons. The sentence should be softened or supported by an ablation over width/depth or a theoretical argument.

comments.title: MNIST input dimension appears inconsistent with the dataset
comments.quote: with 10 neurons per layer (except at the first layer _n_ 0 = 764)
comments.explanation: If this specifies the input dimension of the fully connected MNIST classifier, it is inconsistent with standard MNIST images, whose flattened input dimension is 28×28 = 784, not 764. This could be an OCR or typographical error, since 784 could plausibly be misread as 764. However, if the paper actually states 764, then either the preprocessing must be explained as removing 20 pixels/features, or the network dimension contradicts the dataset. As written, the architecture cannot be reproduced unambiguously.

comments.title: CIFAR/ResNet use of TMAF is underspecified for convolutional feature maps
comments.quote: For the CIFAR-10 dataset, we use the ResNet18 network structure provided by (He et al., 2015a). The activation functions are still ReLU and the diagonal TMAF used in Subsection 3.1.
comments.explanation: The earlier definition of diagonal TMAF is given for vector-valued fully connected layers, with a layerwise scalar step function \(\alpha_\ell\) applied to preactivation coordinates. In a ResNet, activations are convolutional feature maps with channel and spatial dimensions, plus residual additions. The passage does not specify how the diagonal matrix activation is instantiated in this setting: whether \(\alpha_\ell\) is shared across all channels and spatial positions, learned per channel, learned per residual block, applied before or after batch normalization, or whether every ReLU in the ResNet is replaced. These choices substantially affect both parameter count and performance. Saying only that the diagonal TMAF from Subsection 3.1 is used is not enough to reproduce the CIFAR experiments.

comments.title: Claim that the ResNet parameters are already ReLU-tuned is not sufficiently supported
comments.quote: Those parameters given in (Paszke et al., 2019) are already tuned well with respect to ReLU.
comments.explanation: The authors appear to argue that the comparison is not biased in favor of TMAF because the baseline architecture/hyperparameters are standard for ReLU. However, Paszke et al. (2019) is the PyTorch systems paper, not a canonical source of carefully tuned CIFAR-10/CIFAR-100 ResNet hyperparameters. The reported CIFAR-10 accuracies around 77–80% are also well below commonly reported tuned ResNet results on CIFAR-10, suggesting that the training setup may not be strongly optimized. Using the same optimizer and learning rate for all activations is fair in one sense, but it does not establish that the ReLU baseline is tuned well. This weakens the interpretation of the reported TMAF advantage unless the exact recipe, data augmentation, normalization, epoch count, and baseline tuning procedure are provided or a more appropriate citation is used.

comments.title: CIFAR-100 performance claim lacks a reported numerical evaluation in the table
comments.quote: For the CIFAR-100 dataset, we use the ResNet34 network structure provided by (He et al., 2015a) with the ReLU and TMAF activation functions in Subsection 3.1. Numerical results are presented in Figures 9a and 9b. In the training process, TMAF again outperforms the classical ReLU network.
comments.explanation: The paper says it tests MNIST, CIFAR-10, and CIFAR-100, and Figure 9 apparently shows training loss and classification accuracy for CIFAR-100. However, Table 3 reports evaluation accuracy only for MNIST and CIFAR-10. The authors phrase the CIFAR-100 sentence as “in the training process,” so it may be intentionally limited to training curves. Still, for an image classification benchmark, the relevant comparison is usually held-out/test accuracy, not just training loss or training accuracy. Without a numerical CIFAR-100 evaluation accuracy, preferably with variance over runs, the statement that TMAF outperforms ReLU on CIFAR-100 is under-supported and could be read as a general benchmark-performance claim.

comments.title: Adjusting intervals during evaluation is methodologically ambiguous
comments.quote: A simple strategy is to let those intervals in equation 6 be varying and adjusted in the training (or the evaluation) process, which will be investigated in our future research.
comments.explanation: Allowing the interval grid points in \(\alpha_\ell\) to be learned during training is a natural extension, although it raises differentiability and ordering constraints that would need to be handled. The concerning phrase is “or the evaluation process.” In standard supervised learning, model parameters should be fixed during evaluation; changing activation intervals using evaluation/test data would constitute test-time adaptation and could invalidate benchmark comparisons unless clearly separated from the held-out test set or framed as an allowed adaptation procedure. Since this is presented only as future work, the issue is not central to the current experiments, but the wording is ambiguous enough to mislead.

progressive_original__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive Original (gpt-5.5)
progressive_original__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper proposes an interesting and potentially useful idea—learning layer-wise, piecewise-defined multiplicative activation parameters that generalize ReLU/Leaky ReLU—but the current presentation raises substantial concerns about novelty, clarity, and technical rigor. Conceptually, the “matrix activation” terminology seems overstated in the initial construction, since the activation is diagonal and effectively amounts to a trainable piecewise-linear scalar activation of the form \(x \mapsto \alpha(x)x\); the relationship to existing adaptive/piecewise activations such as PReLU, APL, maxout, splines, rational activations, and PWLU needs a much sharper and more convincing comparison. The mathematical exposition is also difficult to follow: notation is often corrupted or inconsistent, equations are missing or poorly rendered, and key definitions—especially how the step parameters are trained, whether thresholds are fixed or learnable, and how gradients behave at discontinuities—are not explained with sufficient precision. Several claims are too strong or unsupported, e.g., that the method is “clearly not worse” than ReLU because it contains ReLU as a special case; this ignores optimization dynamics, parameterization effects, regularization, and possible instability from discontinuous activations. The paper would benefit from a clearer statement of the actual contribution, a principled analysis of expressivity/optimization/gradient behavior, and careful empirical controls against close baselines with comparable parameter counts. Overall, the idea may be worth exploring, but in its current form the beginning of the paper lacks the precision and positioning needed for a strong ICLR submission.

comments.title: Dying ReLU is conflated with the broader vanishing-gradient problem
comments.quote: A drawback of ReLU is the presence of vanishing gradient in the training process, known as dying ReLU problem (Lu et al., 2019).
comments.explanation: I understand the authors' intent: they are motivating trainable activations by pointing to a well-known weakness of ReLU. However, the wording merges two related but distinct phenomena. The standard “vanishing gradient” problem usually refers to gradients becoming very small through repeated chain-rule multiplication in deep networks, especially with saturating nonlinearities. The “dying ReLU” problem is more specific: a ReLU unit can enter a regime where its preactivation is negative for essentially all inputs, so its local derivative is zero and the unit stops updating. ReLU was in fact popular partly because it mitigates classical saturation-based vanishing gradients on the positive side. The sentence is understandable as informal motivation, but as written it could mislead readers about the mechanism being addressed.

comments.title: “Arbitrary trainable matrix activation functions” overstates the construction presented here
comments.quote: In this paper, we shall generalize ReLU and introduce arbitrary trainable matrix activation functions.
comments.explanation: The high-level goal is clear, and later context does discuss going beyond diagonal matrices to tridiagonal and potentially sparse/full matrix operators. Still, in the formal construction in this passage the activation is specifically a diagonal matrix whose entries are generated by a layerwise scalar step function with fixed grid points and trainable step values. That is a useful and flexible family, but it is not “arbitrary” in the usual mathematical sense: the entries are constrained by the chosen piecewise-constant parameterization, are shared through \(\alpha_\ell\), and the grid is later fixed in experiments. The later discussion of more general matrices partly softens the concern, but the wording here overclaims what has actually been defined and trained.

comments.title: Piecewise-constant approximation claim needs domain and norm qualifications
comments.quote: Since piecewise constant functions can approximate a continuous function within arbitrarily high accuracy, we choose _αℓ_ to be the following step function
comments.explanation: I can see the intended justification: step functions are a natural, computationally cheap way to parameterize the multiplier \(\alpha_\ell\), and on compact intervals they can approximate continuous functions uniformly if the partition is sufficiently fine. However, the statement as written is too broad. Uniform approximation of an arbitrary continuous function on all of \(\mathbb{R}\) by a finite piecewise-constant function is not generally possible without additional assumptions, and the relevant approximation norm is not specified. In the actual construction \(\alpha_\ell\) is defined on an unbounded real line with finitely many intervals, including tails, so the standard compact-domain result does not apply directly. This is fixable by saying, for example, “on a compact range of preactivations” or “in an empirical/Lp sense over the data distribution.”

comments.title: Hypothesis-class inclusion does not imply practical performance is “clearly not worse”
comments.quote: Since ReLU and Leaky ReLU are included by our DNN as special cases, the proposed DNN is clearly not worse than the traditional ones in practice.
comments.explanation: The authors are right that, at the level of representable functions, a parameterized family containing ReLU and Leaky ReLU has at least the same best-case approximation capacity, assuming the network architecture and optimization objective are otherwise comparable. What does not follow is the practical claim. Neural-network training is nonconvex, and adding trainable activation parameters can change optimization dynamics, initialization sensitivity, regularization behavior, and effective learning rates. Therefore, inclusion of ReLU/Leaky ReLU as special cases only supports a statement about the optimal attainable loss over the enlarged function class, not that the trained model will be no worse “in practice.” The empirical sections may provide evidence, but the logical implication here is overstated.

comments.title: Activation operator is not generally a map from continuous functions to continuous functions
comments.quote: First we note that _Dℓ_ could be viewed as a nonlinear operator _Tℓ_ : [ _C_ (R _[d]_ )] _[n][ℓ] →_ [ _C_ (R _[d]_ )] _[n][ℓ]_ , where
comments.explanation: The authors are trying to reinterpret the diagonal activation as an operator acting on vector-valued layer functions, which is a useful abstraction for moving from diagonal to tri-diagonal or full activation matrices. However, I checked this against the earlier definition of \(\alpha_\ell\): it is a trainable step function, and the paper explicitly emphasizes that TMAF can include discontinuous activation functions. If \(u\in C(\mathbb R^d)\) crosses one of the step thresholds \(s_j\), then \(\alpha_\ell(u(x))u(x)\) is generally discontinuous unless special compatibility conditions happen to hold at that threshold. Thus \(T_\ell\) does not, in general, map \([C(\mathbb R^d)]^{n_\ell}\) into itself. The intended operator could instead be defined on all functions, measurable functions, or piecewise-continuous functions, but as written the function-space statement conflicts with the discontinuous activations allowed elsewhere.

comments.title: Sparse matrix cost claim needs bounded sparsity, not just sparsity
comments.quote: In fact, if a sparse matrix _D_ is used, so that the matrix-vector multiplication is proportional to the number of rows in the matrix, then the resulting algorithm will be computationally efficient.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended point: diagonal, tri-diagonal, or fixed-band activation matrices can keep the activation cost linear in the layer width. That is true for matrices with a uniformly bounded number of nonzeros per row, such as \(p\)-diagonal matrices with fixed \(p\). But the sentence says this follows from using a sparse matrix. In standard numerical linear algebra, sparse matrix-vector multiplication costs \(O(\operatorname{nnz}(D))\), not automatically \(O(n_\ell)\). A sparse matrix can still have, for example, \(O(n_\ell\log n_\ell)\) or \(O(n_\ell^{3/2})\) nonzeros. The following sentence about \(p\)-diagonal and prescribed sparsity patterns partly clarifies the intent, but the cost statement should explicitly require \(O(n_\ell)\) nonzeros or bounded row degree.

comments.title: Robustness and accuracy conclusion is stronger than the evidence in this section
comments.quote: Clearly, based on the simple examples we have considered here, one may conclude that such possibilities, when investigated in depth, can increase the robustness and the accuracy of the TMAF-NN.
comments.explanation: The authors appropriately hedge that these possibilities need to be investigated in depth, and the examples do suggest that richer activation matrices may be promising. Still, the word "Clearly" and the conclusion about increased robustness and accuracy are not fully supported by the material here. At this point the paper has described the possibility of diagonal, tri-diagonal, and sparse patterns, and later experiments include only a limited set of comparisons. Robustness is also not formally defined in this passage. A more defensible wording would be that these possibilities may improve robustness and accuracy and merit further study, rather than saying one may conclude this from the simple examples.

comments.title: Extension to arbitrary activations is not generally a matrix-vector activation in the same sense
comments.quote: _Our observation also applies to an activation function σ other than ReLU. For example, we may rescale σ_ ( _x_ ) _to obtain σ_ ( _ωi,ℓx_ ) _using a set of constants {ωi,ℓ}_ 1 _≤i≤nℓ,_ 1 _≤ℓ≤L varying layer by layer and neuron by neuron.
comments.explanation: The intended idea seems to be a trainable per-neuron rescaling of a standard activation, which is a reasonable adaptive-activation construction. However, the earlier matrix-activation observation for ReLU works because \(\operatorname{ReLU}(t)=d(t)t\) with \(d(t)\in\{0,1\}\). For a general activation \(\sigma\), representing \(\sigma(\omega t)\) as a diagonal matrix times the input would require a diagonal entry \(\sigma(\omega t)/t\), which may be undefined at \(t=0\) and cannot represent activations with \(\sigma(0)\ne 0\) in the form \(D(t)t\). Many common activations mentioned earlier, such as Softplus or sigmoid-type functions, do not naturally fit this without extra qualifications. If the authors only mean ordinary componentwise scaled activations, that is fine, but then it is not the same matrix-vector observation unless the necessary conditions are stated.

comments.title: Training protocol omits key reproducibility details
comments.quote: In each experiment, we use the same learning rates, stochastic optimization methods, and number NE of epochs (optimization iterations). In particular, the learning rate is 1e-4 from epoch 1 to[NE] 2[and 1e-5 is used from epoch][NE] 2[+ 1][ to][ NE][.][The optimization] method is ADAM (Kingma & Ba (2015)) based on mini-batches of size 500.
comments.explanation: The passage gives useful high-level training information: optimizer, batch size, and learning-rate schedule. I looked for whether the missing details are resolved nearby, but in the provided context the actual value of \(NE\) is not specified, and neither are initializations for the trainable step values \(t_{\ell,j}\). For TMAF, these initialization choices can materially affect optimization, especially because the interval assignments are non-smooth and only the step values are trained. For the image experiments, preprocessing, augmentation, and weight initialization are also important for reproducing CIFAR/MNIST results. The omission does not invalidate the method, but it makes the numerical comparisons difficult to reproduce or assess fairly.

comments.title: Gaussian equal-probability interval claim is numerically inaccurate as stated
comments.quote: For TMAF _Dℓ_ in equation 5, the function _αℓ_ uses intervals ( _−∞, −_ 1 _._ 4), ( _−_ 1 _._ 4 _, −_ 0 _._ 92], ( _−_ 0 _._ 92 _, −_ 0 _._ 56], ( _−_ 0 _._ 56 _, −_ 0 _._ 26], ( _−_ 0 _._ 26 _,_ 0], (0 _,_ 0 _._ 26], (0 _._ 26 _,_ 0 _._ 56], (0 _._ 56 _,_ 0 _._ 92], (0 _._ 92 _,_ 1 _._ 4], (1 _._ 4 _, ∞_ ) such that probability over each of the ten intervals is 0.1 with respect to Gaussian distribution.
comments.explanation: The intent is clear: choose grid intervals roughly matched to Gaussian quantiles, likely because BatchNorm makes preactivations approximately standardized. But the listed cut points are not the standard normal deciles. Equal 0.1-probability bins would use approximate cutoffs \(\pm1.2816\), \(\pm0.8416\), \(\pm0.5244\), \(\pm0.2533\), and 0. The provided values are close in the middle but noticeably off in the tails: for example, \(P(Z>1.4)\approx0.081\), not 0.1. If these are only heuristic rounded bins, the prose should say approximately; if exact equal-mass Gaussian bins are intended, the cutoffs should be corrected.

comments.explanation: Ignoring the OCR interleaving with Table 1, the relevant experimental setup is that the diagonal TMAF, ReLU, and parametric ReLU oscillatory-function networks use 400 neurons per hidden layer, while the tri-diagonal TMAF uses 300. The authors may have intended this to balance cost, since tri-diagonal activations add off-diagonal interactions, and the later table reports both TMAF variants separately. Still, the passage does not explain whether parameter counts, FLOPs, or wall-clock costs are being controlled. TMAF also adds trainable activation parameters relative to ReLU/parametric ReLU. The comparison is suggestive, but without reporting parameter counts and a rationale for the different widths, it is hard to attribute the observed errors solely to the activation mechanism.

comments.title: MNIST input dimension appears inconsistent with the dataset
comments.quote: with 10 neurons per layer (except at the first layer _n_ 0 = 764)
comments.explanation: I first read this as specifying the input dimension of the fully connected MNIST classifier. For standard MNIST images, the flattened input dimension is 28×28 = 784, not 764. I considered whether this could be an OCR error, since 784 could plausibly be misread as 764, and the rest of the context clearly indicates ordinary MNIST. If the intended value is 784, there is no substantive issue. However, if the paper actually states 764, this would be a real numerical inconsistency: either the preprocessing must be explained as removing 20 pixels/features, or the network dimension contradicts the dataset. As written, a careful reader cannot reproduce the architecture unambiguously.

comments.title: Oscillatory-function table conflates training loss with approximation error
comments.quote: Table 2: Error comparison for sin(100 _πx_ ) + cos(50 _πx_ ) + sin( _πx_ )
comments.explanation: The surrounding text uses Table 2 to support claims about approximation quality. I checked the table heading in context, which reports “final loss,” and Figure 4b is described as a training loss comparison. Thus the numbers appear to be final training losses, not necessarily test errors or approximation errors over the domain. This matters because the paper’s formal loss in equation 3 is a sum over the training data, while the table values such as 1.00 look more like a normalized MSE or averaged loss. The authors may indeed have evaluated visually on a grid in Figures 5–6, but the metric in Table 2 is not defined precisely enough to justify calling it an “error comparison” for function approximation. The issue is fixable by stating whether the loss is averaged MSE, summed squared error, training error, or test/grid error.

comments.title: Claim that ReLU networks clearly need many more neurons is stronger than the evidence shown
comments.quote: To capture the high frequency, ReLU-type neural networks are clearly required to use much more neurons, introducing significantly amount of weight and bias parameters.
comments.explanation: The empirical observation is plausible: piecewise-linear ReLU networks often need many linear regions to represent high-frequency oscillations accurately, and the shown ReLU/Para-ReLU runs fail on this target. I checked whether the context provides a theoretical lower bound or a systematic width/depth study; it does not. The claim is based on one architecture and training setup, so the failure could also reflect optimization, initialization, learning-rate schedule, depth/width choice, or insufficient training rather than an inherent requirement for “much more neurons.” Standard approximation theory supports that ReLU networks can approximate oscillatory functions, but the quantitative parameter requirement depends on architecture and accuracy target. The sentence should be softened or supported by an ablation over width/depth or a theoretical argument.

comments.title: Reference to Figure 4b as evidence of approximation quality is imprecise
comments.quote: the diagonal TMAF and tri-diagonal TMAF produce high-quality approximations (see Figures 5 and 4b)
comments.explanation: Figure 5 directly shows the TMAF approximations, so that part supports the statement. But Figure 4b is labeled “Training loss comparison,” not an approximation plot. A decreasing or smaller training loss is relevant evidence, but it is not the same as showing approximation quality, especially if the intended claim is about the function on the whole interval. This is a minor but real cross-reference ambiguity: readers may look at Figure 4b expecting approximation curves. Referring only to Figure 5 for visual approximation quality, and to Figure 4b/Table 2 for training loss, would make the support clearer.

comments.title: CIFAR/ResNet use of TMAF is underspecified for convolutional feature maps
comments.quote: For the CIFAR-10 dataset, we use the ResNet18 network structure provided by (He et al., 2015a). The activation functions are still ReLU and the diagonal TMAF used in Subsection 3.1.
comments.explanation: The earlier definition of diagonal TMAF is given for vector-valued fully connected layers, with a layerwise scalar step function \(\alpha_\ell\) applied to preactivation coordinates. In a ResNet, activations are convolutional feature maps with channel and spatial dimensions, plus residual additions. I looked for whether the passage specifies how the diagonal matrix activation is instantiated in this setting: whether \(\alpha_\ell\) is shared across all channels and spatial positions, learned per channel, learned per residual block, applied before or after batch normalization, and whether every ReLU in the ResNet is replaced. These choices substantially affect both parameter count and performance. Saying only that the “diagonal TMAF used in Subsection 3.1” is used is not enough for reproduction of the CIFAR experiments.

comments.title: Claim that the ResNet parameters are already ReLU-tuned is not sufficiently supported
comments.quote: Those parameters given in (Paszke et al., 2019) are already tuned well with respect to ReLU.
comments.explanation: I understand the authors’ intent: they want to argue that the comparison is not biased in favor of TMAF, because the baseline architecture/hyperparameters are standard for ReLU. However, Paszke et al. is the PyTorch paper, not a canonical source of a carefully tuned CIFAR-10 ResNet18 training recipe. The reported CIFAR-10 accuracies around 77–80% are also well below commonly reported tuned ResNet results on CIFAR-10, suggesting that the training setup may not be strongly optimized. Using the same optimizer and learning rate for all activations is fair in one sense, but it does not establish that the ReLU baseline is tuned well. This weakens the interpretation of the reported TMAF advantage unless the exact recipe, data augmentation, normalization, epoch count, and baseline tuning procedure are provided.

comments.title: CIFAR-100 performance claim lacks a reported numerical evaluation in the table
comments.quote: For the CIFAR-100 dataset, we use the ResNet34 network structure provided by (He et al., 2015a) with the ReLU and TMAF activation functions in Subsection 3.1. Numerical results are presented in Figures 9a and 9b. In the training process, TMAF again outperforms the classical ReLU network.
comments.explanation: The paper says it tests MNIST, CIFAR-10, and CIFAR-100, and Figure 9 apparently shows training loss and classification accuracy for CIFAR-100. However, Table 3 reports evaluation accuracy only for MNIST and CIFAR-10. The authors carefully phrase the CIFAR-100 sentence as “in the training process,” so it may be intentionally limited to training curves. Still, for an image classification benchmark, the relevant comparison is usually held-out/test accuracy, not just training loss or training accuracy. Without a numerical CIFAR-100 evaluation accuracy, preferably with variance over runs, the statement that TMAF outperforms ReLU on CIFAR-100 is under-supported and could be read as a general benchmark-performance claim.

comments.title: Adjusting intervals during evaluation is methodologically ambiguous
comments.quote: A simple strategy is to let those intervals in equation 6 be varying and adjusted in the training (or the evaluation) process, which will be investigated in our future research.
comments.explanation: Allowing the interval grid points in \(\alpha_\ell\) to be learned during training is a natural extension, although it raises differentiability and ordering constraints that would need to be handled. What concerned me is the phrase “or the evaluation process.” In standard supervised learning, model parameters should be fixed during evaluation; changing activation intervals using evaluation/test data would constitute test-time adaptation and could invalidate benchmark comparisons unless clearly separated from the held-out test set or framed as an allowed adaptation procedure. Since this is presented only as future work, the issue is not central to the current experiments, but the wording is ambiguous enough to mislead.

comments.title: PyTorch reference does not support the paper’s stated source of tuned ResNet parameters
comments.quote: - Adam Paszke, Sam Gross, Francisco Massa, Adam Lerer, James Bradbury, Gregory Chanan, Trevor Killeen, Zeming Lin, Natalia Gimelshein, Luca Antiga, Alban Desmaison, Andreas Kopf, Edward Yang, Zachary DeVito, Martin Raison, Alykhan Tejani, Sasank Chilamkurthy, Benoit Steiner, Lu Fang, Junjie Bai, and Soumith Chintala. Pytorch: An imperative style, high-performance deep learning library. In H. Wallach, H. Larochelle, A. Beygelzimer, F. d'Alch´e-Buc, E. Fox, and R. Garnett (eds.), _Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems_ , volume 32. Curran Associates, Inc., 2019. URL https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper/2019/file/ bdbca288fee7f92f2bfa9f7012727740-Paper.pdf.
comments.explanation: The bibliographic entry itself is a standard reference for PyTorch, and it is appropriate for supporting the statement that the experiments were implemented in PyTorch. However, in the surrounding paper context the authors also say that the ResNet/CIFAR parameters from Paszke et al. are already well tuned for ReLU. I checked whether this reference could plausibly support that claim, but Paszke et al. (2019) is the PyTorch systems paper, not the ResNet architecture paper or a source of tuned CIFAR-10/CIFAR-100 ResNet hyperparameters. The ResNet architecture is properly associated elsewhere with He et al. (2015a), but the specific claim about tuned parameters needs either a different citation, an explicit statement that the authors used a particular PyTorch example/tutorial implementation, or enough hyperparameter details to make the setup reproducible. As written, the reference is correct for PyTorch but not for the methodological claim it appears to be asked to support.
